title: We Went to Cannes! These Are the 10 Best (and Worst) Things We Saw.
format: Sean Fennessey and Amanda Dobbins record from Cannes on Amanda's last morning, recapping their first festival: what they loved, what flopped, and what left them baffled, plus Palme d'Or predictions.

films.title: Club Kid
films.note: Jordan Firstman's Un Certain Regard debut, Sean's favorite of the festival. Set among club kids in 2016 New York, it pivots from in-your-face comedy to a sincere drama about parenthood. Produced in part by Alex Coco (Anora), acquired by A24.

films.title: Fjord
films.note: Cristian Mungiu's new film and Sean's pre-festival Palme pick. Sebastian Stan and Renate Reinsve as a religious Romanian family that immigrates to progressive Norway. Sean's read: this year's Anatomy of a Fall.

films.title: All of a Sudden
films.note: Ryusuke Hamaguchi's 196-minute film about elder care, capitalism and human connection, with Virginie Efira. Includes a 12-minute whiteboard lecture on capitalism; both hosts were dubious at the midpoint and in tears by the end.

films.title: Paper Tiger
films.note: James Gray's Queens crime drama, set in 1986, with Adam Driver, Miles Teller and Scarlett Johansson. Sean, who finds Gray's world eerily close to his own family's, calls it near his best; from Neon in the fall.

films.title: Minotaur
films.note: Andrey Zvyagintsev's return, a remake of Chabrol's The Unfaithful Wife reset against the Russia-Ukraine war. Plays as a thriller for 90 minutes, then turns overtly metaphorical. Iris Lebedeva and Anatoly Belay star.

films.title: Teenage Sex and Death at Camp Miasma
films.note: Jane Schoenbrun's meta-slasher with Gillian Anderson and Hannah Einbinder, in Un Certain Regard. Amanda's favorite Schoenbrun; poppier and more accessible than I Saw the TV Glow. Opens in August.

films.title: Fatherland
films.note: Pawel Pawlikowski's first film since Cold War, black-and-white, about Thomas Mann and his daughter Erika returning to Germany after WWII. Sub-75 minutes, with Sandra Huller and August Diehl. Leading the Cannes critics' poll.

films.title: Nagi Notes
films.note: Koji Fukada's quiet, play-based drama set in a small Japanese town near a military base. About artists, lost loves and self-discovery; Amanda's word for it was 'lovely.'

films.title: The Samurai and the Prisoner
films.note: Kiyoshi Kurosawa's first samurai film, out of competition. A 15th-century samurai lord plays Sherlock Holmes to a captured enemy's Watson. Masahiro Motoki and Masaki Suda star; borderline rapturous reviews.

films.title: Parallel Tales
films.note: Asghar Farhadi's French-language film, a soft adaptation of Kieslowski, with Isabelle Huppert, Vincent Cassel, Virginie Efira and Catherine Deneuve. In competition and, both hosts agreed, a dull misuse of its stars.

films.title: Sheep in the Box
films.note: Hirokazu Kore-eda's in-competition film about grieving parents who replace their dead son with an AI double. Sean found it wandering and vague; Amanda skipped it on his warning.

films.title: The Last Interview
films.note: Steven Soderbergh's documentary on John Lennon's final interview, given to three San Francisco radio hosts the day he died. Torpedoed for both hosts by cheap AI-generated animation.

films.title: Colony
films.note: Yeon Sang-ho (Train to Busan) on hive-mind, ant-colony zombies. Sean liked the core idea but found no characters of interest and leaden storytelling.

films.title: Butterfly Jam
films.note: Kantemir Balagov (Beanpole) in Directors' Fortnight, on masculinity in a Circassian immigrant community in New Jersey. With Barry Keoghan, Harry Melling and Riley Keough. Uneven; panned early.

films.title: Hope
films.note: Na Hong-jin's (The Wailing) big-budget alien-invasion thriller, incongruously in competition. A 45-minute pre-CGI opening set piece the hosts loved, plus rough monster effects and a 2-hour-40 runtime. Michael Fassbender, Alicia Vikander and Taylor Russell.

films.title: The Devils
films.note: Ken Russell's scandalous 1971 film, with Oliver Reed and Vanessa Redgrave, screened in Russell's director's cut as the first release from Warner's new Clockwork imprint. A festival highlight for Sean; U.S. theaters in October.

films.title: The Unknown
films.note: Arthur Harari (co-writer of Anatomy of a Fall) on a body-and-soul swap triggered by sex with a stranger. Grim, Lynch- and Bunuel-adjacent; Lea Seydoux stars with about 25 lines of dialogue. Divisive.

films.title: Bitter Christmas
films.note: Pedro Almodovar back in Spanish, already open in Europe. A fiasco for 90 minutes, then a hysterical final 15 about inspiration and where an artist's ideas come from. Barbara Lenny in the cast.

films.title: The Beloved
films.note: Rodrigo Sorogoyen's film with Javier Bardem as a director casting his estranged adult daughter. Amanda's label: 'normie Sentimental Value,' saved by one strong making-of set piece.

films.title: A Woman's Life
films.note: Charline Bourgeois-Tacquet on a surgeon weighing how much she wants to work versus live. Fine, not bad; structured in chapters, one titled 'having it all.'

films.title: Gentle Monster
films.note: Marie Kreutzer (Corsage) with Lea Seydoux as a mother whose husband is arrested on child-pornography charges, and Catherine Deneuve. Amanda found it grim and provocative, but ultimately hollow.

films.title: Avedon
films.note: Ron Howard's documentary portrait of fashion photographer Richard Avedon, presented by Howard at Cannes. Standard but comprehensive, and mercifully AI-free.

Teenage Sex and Death at Camp Miasma.title: Teenage Sex and Death at Camp Miasma
